source_metadata.title_raw: Michael C. Ghizzoni  - A-19-001 - February 4, 2019 - Santa Barbara

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 4, 2019

Michael C. Ghizzoni
Santa Barbara County Counsel
105 E. Anapamu Street, Suite 201
Santa Barbara, CA 93101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-001

Dear Mr. Ghizzoni:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Barbara County Supervisor Peter Adam regarding conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

May Supervisor Adam participate in decisions regarding the North Fork Ranch Frost Ponds Project to be located 4,000 to 6,000 feet from the Adams Family Cuyama Ranch, in which Supervisor Adam and his immediate family have an ownership interest, when the parcel on which the project is located is adjacent to the ranch property?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The facts do not indicate a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon any of Supervisor Adam's financial interests as a result of the decisions.

_____________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-001
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Project at issue: Applicant Brodiaca, Inc. seeks a minor conditional use permit to construct
three frost ponds or reservoirs on the North Fork Ranch Vineyards property, a 6,565-acre property
zoned agricultural in northeast Santa Barbara County, with 1,000 acres of vineyards. This
application includes a request for adoption of a draft Mitigated Negative Declaration under the
California Environmental Quality Act, ("CEQA"). The three frost ponds would each have the
capacity to store up to 49 acre feet of water to be used for frost protection. These types of reservoirs
are an allowed use within this agricultural zone, with a minor conditional use permit.

The Applicant is already conducting frost protection on its vineyards, using agricultural
wells and pumps. This involves sustained spray irrigation on the grapevines when frost warnings
occur in the months of February through April. It is not anticipated that the reservoirs would
involve a greater water use for frost protection; however, there will be new evaporation losses of
water, estimated at 8 acre feet per year, for all three ponds. The current reservoir sites are vacant
and have been historically used for grazing livestock. The frost ponds would be supplied by existing
agricultural wells through existing vineyard irrigation pipelines that extend beneath the nearby
highway and throughout the vineyard. The project also includes the construction of new
underground pipelines that would extend between each of the proposed reservoirs and the existing
vineyard irrigation system.

In a telephone conversation on January 22, 2018, Jenna Richardson, Deputy County
Counsel, indicated that the applicant seeks to construct the reservoirs so the water is available and
ready to be used in the event of a frost. This avoids placing a strain on irrigation pumps in a time of
urgency. The reservoirs would be maintained at a full condition between February and April. A
maximum of three feet of well-supplied water would be maintained in the reservoirs the remainder
of the year and used for vineyard irrigation.

Groundwater Context: The proposed reservoir sites are located in the western portion of the
Cuyama Valley Groundwater Basin. The 2014 Groundwater Basins Status Report indicates that
groundwater level measurements in the Cuyama Valley Groundwater Basin show substantial
declines throughout history and over the last three years.

Procedural Background: The County's zoning staff approved the applicant's Minor
Conditional Use Permit and adopted the draft Mitigated Negative Declaration. Neighbors appealed
this matter to the Planning Commission, requesting that the County evaluate the reservoirs and the
vineyards as a single project, make a finding that the project's use of groundwater would be
detrimental to the Cuyama area, and that the reservoirs and vineyard contribute to groundwater
overdraft in the area.² Ms. Richardson explained that the appeal asserted that a full Environmental
Impact Report ("EIR") for the ponds and the vineyard was required under CEQA, rather than the
Mitigated Negative Declaration.³

2 The Zoning Staff Report is available: http://www.sbcountyplanning.org/PDF/boards/za/09-25-2017/16CUP-
00000-00005/Staff%20Report.pdf, as viewed January 23, 2019.

3 Per Ms. Richardson, an EIR would provide a more in depth report on the environmental issues.

File No. A-19-001
Page No. 3

The Planning Commission determined that the project requires only a discretionary permit
and accompanying review level under CEQA requirements. The Planning Commission approved
the applicant's Conditional Use Permit and adopted the Mitigated Negative Declaration. The
Planning Commission determined that the vineyard and the proposed frost ponds are separate
projects under CEQA, and that only the impacts of the reservoirs, not the vineyards that were
installed in prior years, must be examined.⁴ Looking to the evaporation issue, the draft Mitigated
Negative Declaration for the project states that the project-related physical change in existing
groundwater conditions would result in a "less than significant" land use impact.⁵ Ms. Richardson
noted that the decision made before the Board on this application is not a water rights issue. This
application for the frost pond project is now on appeal to the County Board of Supervisors.

Supervisor Adam's Interests: Supervisor Adam and his immediate family (spouse and
children) own and manage business entity Cascavel Holdings, LLC ("Cascavel"). Cascavel has
62.5% ownership in approximately 2,800 acres of property known as Adam Family Cuyama Ranch
("Ranch"). The parcels owned by the Ranch share a property line with the adjacent project to the
east for approximately 17,675 feet. Ms. Richardson states that the nearest reservoir to the property
line will be 4,000 to 6,000 feet away. She noted there is not an odor or visibility issue related to the
frost ponds and neighboring properties. The Ranch is currently leased to a cattle operator for
approximately $25,000 a year. Cascavel receives 6.25% of the yearly lease income from the Ranch.
Two groundwater wells are located on the Ranch properties that are used for the cattle operation
and draw water from the Cuyama Valley Groundwater Basin.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The financial interests
implicated by your facts are:

• A business entity interest, where an official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more in a business entity (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is
a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87103(d)).

• A real property interest, where an official has a direct or indirect interest of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(b)) including a pro rata share of interests in real
property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate

_____________
⁴ The County Planning Commission information is available:
http://www.sbcountyplanning.org/PDF/boards/CntyPC/09-12-2018/17APL-00000-00017/Staff%20Presentation.pdf. As
viewed January 23, 2019.

⁵ The Planning Commission further found that water wells in the Cuyama Valley will be subject to the
requirements of the Sustainable Groundwater Management Act ("SGMA") once the required plan is adopted by the
local Groundwater Sustainability Agency.

File No. A-19-001
Page No. 4

family owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater
(Section 82033).

• An interest in a source of income, aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or
more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) This
includes any community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata
share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or
her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater
interest. (Section 82030(a).)

Based on the fact provided, Supervisor Adam has interests in Cascavel as a business entity
and as a source of income. He also has a real property interest in the Ranch properties held by
Cascavel.

Foreseeability and Materiality

The standard for foreseeability varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly
involved in the decision.⁶ For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at
issue, the financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can
be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation
18701(b).) Supervisor Adam's interests are not explicitly involved in the decisions at issue.

Business Entities and Source of Income

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a financial effect is material if a prudent person with
sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect
would contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business entity.⁷ This includes
decisions that:

(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity
in which the business entity is engaged;

(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the
business entity is engaged;

(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business
entity supplies;

⁶ An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision if the interest is a named
party in, or subject of, the decision. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
financial interest, or if the decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).
(Regulation 18701(a).)

⁷ Under Regulation 18702.3, the materiality standard for a source of income that is a business entity is the
same as for an interest in the business entity itself.

File No. A-19-001
Page No. 5

(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment
projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either
temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives;

(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other
project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws
from the location of the project; or

(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of
the business entity.

None of the facts indicate that the decisions regarding the frost pond reservoir application
would contribute to a reasonably foreseeable change in the value of Cascavel. Decisions on the
discretionary conditional use permit and the Mitigated Negative Declaration do not affect
Cascavel's business of leasing its land for continued agricultural use. While the objection to the
application raises the issue of groundwater sustainability for the region, the water use for frost
protection is already occurring on the applicant's parcel. The construction of the reservoirs is
anticipated to increase this water use in regards to the amount of evaporation off the ponds. The
draft Mitigated Negative Declaration states that the change in existing groundwater use would result
in a "less than significant" impact.

Real Property

Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standard for real property interests.⁸ While the
parcel on which the reservoirs will be located is adjacent to the Ranch Property, the nearest
proposed frost pond reservoir to the Ranch property line is 4,000 to 6,000 feet away. Where the
governmental decision affects a clearly defined, specific, and isolated site, such as a particular
building on a large tract of land, the Commission has interpreted the materiality regulations to allow
the distance to be measured from that clearly defined and specifically affected portion. (See for
example McLaughlin Advice Letter, No. A-05-061.) It is not anticipated that the reservoirs would
involve a greater water use for frost protection of the existing vineyard than is already occurring,
apart from the issue of evaporation. The draft Mitigated Negative Declaration indicates that the
change in existing groundwater use related to evaporation would result in a "less than significant"
impact. Based upon these facts, it appears that the effect of the reservoir decision is limited to the
clearly defined and specific reservoir sites located 1,000 feet or more from the Ranch property.
Therefore, the applicable materiality standard is stated in Regulation 18702.2(b):

(b) The financial effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in
which an official has a financial interest involving property 1,000 feet or more from
the property line of the official's property is presumed not to be material. This
presumption may be rebutted with clear and convincing evidence the governmental
decision would have a substantial effect on the official's property.

Pursuant to Regulation 18702.2(b), it is presumed that the financial effect on Supervisor

⁸ Regulation 18702.2 was amended by the Commission on January 17, 2019. We apply the amended version of
this regulation for this analysis.

File No. A-19-001
Page No. 6

Adam's real property interest will not be material, unless the presumption is rebutted by clear and convincing evidence of a substantial effect on the official's property. As indicated in the draft Mitigated Negative Declaration, the change in existing groundwater use would result in a "less than significant" impact. Therefore, there is no clear and convincing evidence that the decisions involving the project will have a substantial effect on the Supervisor's property with regard to groundwater use. Additionally, there are no facts indicating that the application's approval or denial will impact the Supervisor's adjacent land by affecting the Ranch's market value, character, use, income producing potential or development potential. Thus, the presumption is not rebutted.

Accordingly, decisions regarding the frost pond reservoir application do not appear to have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on Supervisor Adam's interests and he is not prohibited from taking part in the decisions under the Act.


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

the project is located is adjacent to the ranch property? .

CONCLUSION: Yes. The facts do not indicate a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon any of 
Supervisor Adam's financial interests as a result of the decisions.
